Project Development Objective (PDO)
Original PDO
The project's development objective is to increase the export competitiveness of Moldovan enterprises and
decrease the regulatory burden they face.

This PDO will be achieved through a set of measures that aim to: (i) improve the business environment through
regulatory reforms that reduce the cost of doing business; (ii) help SMEs and exporters to get access to business
development services;and (iii) improve access to medium and long term finance for export oriented enterprises.

I. PROJECT CONTEXT AND DEVELOPMENT OBJECTIVES

A. CONTEXT AT APPRAISAL
Context
A. Country Context
By the early 2000s, Moldova had begun to recover some of the economic losses suffered after the disintegration
of the Soviet Union in the 1990s. Although agriculture and manufacturing continued to struggle, economic
growth was driven by a boom in domestic demand funded by remittances. Between 2000 and 2008, Moldova
became one of the world’s most remittance-dependent countries, with remittances growing from 11.5 percent
of GDP in 2000 to 30 percent by 2008.1 Remittance-fueled consumption, services, and housing were the drivers
of growth in the period leading up to the global financial crisis.

By the mid-2000s, Moldova had reduced poverty and improved shared prosperity, with the national poverty
rate falling from 30.2 percent to 17.5 percent and extreme poverty falling from 4.5 percent to 0.9 percent
between 2006 and 2011. Over the same period, growth in the consumption of the bottom 40 percent of the
population (5.8 percent per year) outpaced average total growth in consumption (2.9 percent per year). Despite
these gains, Moldova remained among the poorest countries in Europe, with a per-capita income of US$2,942 in
2011.

B. Sectoral and Institutional Context
During the 2000s, Moldova’s economy had undergone significant sectoral changes, with the relative
contribution of the service sector increasing in relation to agriculture and manufacturing. This shift, however,
had not translated into job creation, with Moldova's employment to population ratio decreasing from 58 percent
to only 38 percent between 2000 and 2012.

The export sector also exhibited little dynamism, with the country’s exports of goods and services growing at
less than 5 percent annually (in real terms) between 2000 and 2008, a pace slower than overall GDP, and slower
than exports in many other Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS) countries.2 Much of the export growth
had been driven by re-exports (i.e., additional processing of goods using raw materials and design specifications
produced elsewhere), which, unlike direct exports, were shown to contribute little to job creation.3 Direct exports
had remained relatively concentrated in a few traditional industries--mainly fresh fruit and nuts, wine, and
textiles--with little expansion to non-CIS countries.

To successfully compete in higher-quality and higher value-added product markets in the European Union (EU)
and elsewhere, Moldovan enterprises needed to further develop their capacity. Improvements in firms’
strategic management, financial management, product quality, production processes, and other areas were
needed to increase competitiveness. However, information asymmetries about the quality of business services

[http://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/315311468277759007/Moldova-Second-Competitiveness-Enhancement-Project]
2 World Bank (2011), Moldova - After the Global Crisis: Promoting Competitiveness and Shared Growth , Country Economic Memorandum.

2011.
3 World Bank (2011).



                                                                                                                       Page 5 of 58
     The World Bank
    MD Second Competitiveness Enhancement Project (P144103)



that could help exporters make these improvements reduced businesses’ willingness to pay for them. Likewise,
although there had been some reform progress, the business environment in Moldova remained characterized
by uncertainty and high costs in businesses’ interactions with government. In addition, the competitiveness of
Moldovan enterprises, particularly micro, small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs), was also hindered by
their lack of access to finance, as banks had limited access to medium- to long-term funding, tended to lack
suitable products and credit evaluation techniques for loans to smaller companies, and faced general deficiencies
in risk management.

To fuel job-generating economic growth and sustain gains in poverty alleviation, the Government of the
Republic of Moldova (GoM) adopted a policy agenda to support export-led economic growth. This approach
was reflected in the priorities under Moldova 2020 National Development Strategy, including improving the
business enabling environment and promoting better access to finance for enterprises as two of its top priorities.
Concomitantly, the country’s 2014 Roadmap for Increasing Competitiveness focused on the need to improve
competitiveness at the enterprise level. The World Bank had been supporting the GoM in its competitiveness and
reform efforts under the 2006-2013 Competitiveness Enhancement Project (CEP I). This operation, the Second
Competitiveness Enhancement Project (CEP II), provided further Bank support to help the GoM achieve its
objective of re-orienting the economy to export-led growth by increasing the export competitiveness of Moldovan
enterprises, including by increasing access to finance, and reducing the regulatory burden they face.

Theory of Change (Results Chain)

The Project Appraisal Document (PAD) did not describe a Theory of Change (ToC).4 For this Implementation
Completion and Results Report (ICR), the ToC was reconstructed ex-post using information from the PAD and the
Project Results Framework (PRF). As shown on Figure 1, the operation’s design was implicitly underlined by a
Results Chain that linked the three lines of intervention supported by the operation to the Project Development
Objectives (PDOs) of “increasing the export competitiveness of Moldovan enterprises” and “decreasing the
regulatory burden they face.” Specifically, the operation aimed to reduce the regulatory burden faced by
Moldovan enterprises by supporting regulatory reforms (Component 1). It also aimed to increase their export
competitiveness by: helping MSMEs and exporters to get access to business development services (Component
2); and improving access to medium- and long-term finance for export-oriented enterprises (Component 3).




4As per Operations Policy and Country Services (OPCS) guidelines, the ToC became mandatory for PADs as of May 2018. The Project was
approved by the World Bank’s Board of Directors on July 11, 2014.

Project Development Objectives (PDOs)

The operation’s PDOs were: (i) to increase the export competitiveness of Moldovan enterprises; and (ii) to
decrease the regulatory burden they face.

Key Expected Outcomes and Outcome Indicators

Four Key Outcomes Indicators (KOIs) were adopted to assess progress toward PDO achievement:

KOI 1 - Reduction in management time spent meeting regulatory requirements.
KOI 2 - Percentage of matching grant recipients that are engaged in a new export-oriented activity.
KOI 3 - Average annual percentage increase in lending to export-oriented enterprises by participating financial
institutions.

                                                                                                       Page 7 of 58
     The World Bank
    MD Second Competitiveness Enhancement Project (P144103)




KOI 4 - Cumulative amount of medium- and long-term lending by participating financial institutions under the line
of credit.

Components

Component 1 - Regulatory Reform (US$6.24 million, equivalent to 18 percent of total proceeds): This component
aimed to improve Moldova’s business-enabling environment by implementing the regulatory reforms envisioned
by the GoM.5 This component provided technical assistance (TA) under two subcomponents as well as Results-
Based Financing (RBF):
•    Subcomponent 1.A - Reform governance and capacity building: TA under this subcomponent aimed to
     improve the timely delivery of reforms and quality of the business-enabling environment, with a focus on:
     (i) strengthening oversight of reform strategies implementation; (ii) increasing accountability of public
     authorities’ impacts on the business community; (iii) ensuring that laws and regulations do not impose
     unjustified costs on businesses; and (iv) strengthening awareness among public officials of the importance
     of creating a more “business- friendly” culture.
•    Subcomponent 1.B - Reform implementation support: TA under this subcomponent aimed to provide support
     to the GoM in implementing reforms that could greatly benefit export competitiveness, with a focus on: (i)
     streamlining, enhancing transparency, and reducing of permissive documents, including business licenses,
     permits and authorizations through the development of One-Stop electronic platforms; and (ii) competition
     advocacy and implementation capacity building.
•    RBF: Component 1 also included US$1.5 million in RBF contingent upon the achievement of Disbursement-
     Linked Indicators (DLI) deemed to be highly relevant to the success of regulatory reform, including: (i)
     establishing and implementing performance indicators for government agencies with regulatory business
     functions; and (ii) progress in the enactment of reforms to reduce regulatory barriers and remove anti-
     competitive elements of legislation (laws, regulations, or other legal provisions).

Component 2 - Small and Medium Enterprise Development (US$8.04 million, equivalent to 23 percent of total
proceeds): This component aimed to strengthen Moldovan MSMEs’ linkages to markets and their ability to
compete in those markets. Support under this component was provided through TA, matching grants and RBF.
•    Subcomponent 2.A - Institutional Strengthening: This subcomponent provided TA to strengthen the
     institutional capacity of Moldova’s Organization for SME Development (ODIMM) and the Moldovan
     Investment and Export Promotion Agency (MIEPO) to enhance their effectiveness in facilitating market-
     based MSME growth and export-oriented sectors as well as addressing information asymmetries in the
     market for business development and other related services.
•    Subcomponent 2.B - Matching Grants Facility (MGF): This subcomponent provided financial support for the
     preparation and implementation of a MGF to help MSMEs implement activities aimed at enhancing their

5 These strategies included the Roadmap for the GoM’s Actions to Remove Critical Barriers in the Business Environment for 2013- 2014
(Business Roadmap), approved in September 2013; the Regulatory Reform Strategy and the corresponding Action Plan 2013-2020,
approved in December 2013; and the Roadmap for Increasing Competitiveness, approved in January 2014.

      export competitiveness. The US$3 million MGF was expected to help approximately 250 enterprises gain
      access to Business Development Services (BDS) and other relevant business services focused on export
      competitiveness.
•     RBF: Component 2 also included US$1.5 million in RBF contingent upon the achievement of DLIs focusing on
      the public reporting of reform results to encourage accountability and improve performance.

Component 3 - Access to Finance (US$30.00 million, equivalent to 86 percent of total proceeds): This component
aimed to improve access to medium- to long-term finance for export-oriented enterprises, reduce barriers to
finance due to perceived high credit risk in MSME finance and high collateral requirements, and promote suitable
models for value-chain financing, particularly in the agriculture sector. This component included three sub-
components:
•    Subcomponent 3.A - Line of Credit (LoC): This subcomponent provided medium- to long-term financing for
     working capital and investment purposes to export-oriented enterprises. The US$29.4 million LoC was
     expected to benefit approximately 85 private enterprises, including direct and indirect exporters (up to 30
     percent of the LoC for indirect exporters). The LoC was to be on-lent through eligible participating financial
     institutions (PFIs) based on specific eligibility criteria at market-based interest rates based on PFI’s own credit
     risk assessment.
•    Subcomponent 3.B – Risk-Sharing Facility: This subcomponent provided TA to ODIMM for the
     implementation of a time-bound action plan to restructure its existing loan guarantee mechanism, improve
     its governance, and strengthen institutional capacity.
•    Subcomponent 3.C – Value-chain financing: This subcomponent provided TA to relevant government
     authorities for the development of value-chain financing models in coordination with relevant financial
     institutions.

B. SIGNIFICANT CHANGES DURING IMPLEMENTATION
Revised PDOs

The PDOs remained unchanged throughout the operation’s lifetime.

Revised PDO Indicators

The operational definition of KOI 4 (i.e., Cumulative amount of medium- and long-term lending by PFIs under the
LoC) was modified, from being expressed in absolute terms (US$ million) to being expressed in relative terms (as
a percentage of total disbursements under the LoC). Such modification ensured that the indicator accurately
captured the relative share of medium and long-term lending in the LoC portfolio while decoupling it from KOI 3
(i.e., Volume of PFI funding for exporters through the Line of Credit). In other words, the revised operational
definition focused more precisely on the relative share of medium and long-term lending of the LoC, regardless of
the volume of lending under the LoC, which was the focus of KOI 3.

Revised Targets

The end-target for KOI 4 was also adjusted downward from an 80 percent equivalent (US$23.4 million out of
US$29.4 million in the LoC) to 60 percent to better reflect prevailing conditions in the banking sector at the time
of the 2019 restructuring. The banking sector was still recovering from the effects of the large-scale banking fraud,


                                                                                                              Page 9 of 58
     The World Bank
    MD Second Competitiveness Enhancement Project (P144103)



which was uncovered in 2014, leading to the three largest banks losing their banking licenses. As of October 2018,
only 50.2 percent of new loans granted by Moldovan banks had a maturity over 2 years. In that context, the revised
60 percent end-target was still ambitious but also more realistic.

Revised Components

The original components remained unchanged throughout the operation’s lifetime.
Other Changes

First Project Restructuring (2019)6 – A Level 2 (L2) restructuring took place on April 2, 2019. The main modifications
introduced under this restructuring included:
•     Extension of the closing date: It was extended from January 31, 2020, to July 30, 2021, to account for
      implementation delays resulting from a government reorganization and management changes in key
      ministries and agencies; delays in setting-up a market-competitive interest rate for the LoC; and temporary
      slowdown in implementation resulting from the 2019 parliamentary elections.
•     Changes to DLIs:
     - DLI 1 (i.e., Establish and apply performance indicators for Government authorities with business regulatory
         function): While no substantive changes were introduced, the specific reforms and measurements to be
         considered for disbursements after the government reorganization were clarified.
     - DLI 4 (i.e., Adoption and implementation of a Strategy for MIEPO that promotes organizational
         effectiveness and reflects market segmentation and improved export promotion delivery assistance
         mechanisms): MIEPO was replaced by Investment Agency (IA) in the description to reflect newly assigned
         institutional responsibilities under the government reorganization.7
•     Other changes to the PRF: In addition to the change in the operational definition and end-target of KOI 4
      discusses earlier, modifications under the 2019 restructuring were made to some Intermediate Results
      Indicators (IRI), as follows:
     - IRI 2 (i.e., Percentage of laws and sub-laws affecting businesses that are assessed by the Regulatory Impact
         Assessment - RIA Secretariat before approval by Parliament): i) Change in baseline from 80 percent to 35
         percent to correct data entry error; ii) change in target from 95 percent to 75 percent to reflect a more
         realistic level of achievement; and iii) change in name to reflect institutional responsibilities resulting from
         the government reorganization.
     - IRIs 4 and 5: MIEPO was replaced by IA to reflect newly assigned institutional responsibilities under
         government restructuring.

Second Project Restructuring (2021)8 – Another L2 restructuring took place on April 14, 2021, to extend the
operation’s closing for a second time, from July 30, 2021, to December 31, 2021. The five-month extension was
requested by the GoM to complete Project activities that were delayed due to COVID-19 pandemic and political
turmoil in Moldova. The completion of Project activities would, in turn, help achieve the disbursement of funds
and reach end-targets under the PRF.

Rationale for Changes and Their Implication on the Original Theory of Change

The ToC remained unchanged.


II. OUTCOME

A. RELEVANCE OF PDOs
Assessment of Relevance of PDOs and Rating

Rating: High
The operation’s PDOs are in full alignment with the Bank’s FY18-21 Country Partnership Framework (CPF), 9 which
aims to support Moldova’s transition to a new, more sustainable, and inclusive development and growth model.
Specifically, the operation provided direct support under the CPF’s Focus Area 1: Economic Governance –
Strengthening Rule of Law & Accountability in Economic Institutions and, within it, CPF’s Objective 1.A: Enhanced
quality and implementation of investment climate regulation. The continuous relevance of the operation’s PDO
was reaffirmed under the CPF’s mid-term strategy assessment (i.e., Performance and Learning Review) that
validated the CPF’s focus areas and extended it through FY22. PDO 1 also reflected the recommendations from
Moldova's Systematic Country Diagnostic (August 2016),10 which identified the need to improve the business
regulatory framework as one of six priority areas for making progress toward the twin goals of ending extreme
poverty and boosting shared prosperity.

The operation’s PDOs continue to be highly relevant under Moldova’s current government program —"Moldova
of Good Times”—that was presented in August 2021. Specifically, it reaffirms the GoM’s commitment to pursuing
economic policies aimed at promoting exports and access to investment and financial resources. Other top
development goals also reflect full alignment with the operation’s PDO and areas of intervention, including to:
simplify the rules for MSMEs’ operation; foster business growth and internationalization; support MSMEs through
financing programs and entrepreneurial training; and develop a universal legal mechanism for independent
assessment of all state institutions and enterprises and to define relevant performance criteria. “Reducing the

B. ACHIEVEMENT OF PDOs (EFFICACY)
Methodological note - The operation’s efficiency is assessed based on the achievement of two PDOs: PDO 1 - To
decrease the regulatory burden faced by Moldovan enterprises; and PDO 2 - To increase their export competitiveness.
As noted earlier, while the end-target for KOI 4 was adjusted downwards under the 2019 restructuring, actual
achievement under such indicator surpassed both the original and revised end-targets. Thus, the application of the
split evaluation is not warranted.

Assessment of Achievement of Each Objective/Outcome

PDO 1 – To decrease the regulatory burden faced by Moldovan enterprises.

The operation contributed substantially to improving the business enabling environment in Moldova. Specifically,

Institutional Strengthening

The operation contributed to the sustained progress in the enactment of reforms aimed at reducing the
regulatory burden on businesses and streamlining procedures for obtaining permits. In turn, these reforms have
helped reduce the number of direct contacts required between businesses and issuing agencies and moving away
from paper-based applications; thus, enhancing both the efficacy and efficiency of these agencies. In addition,
the operation supported the adoption of performance monitoring indicators to allow government agencies tasked
with reform to gauge their progress, enhancing overall transparency and accountability in public sector
performance. Finally, the operation supported the adoption and implementation of institutional strengthening
strategies for ODIMM and the IA, which have shown improvements in their outreach and effectiveness, as well
as the public reporting of their performance to encourage accountability and transparency.

Mobilizing Private Sector Financing

By fostering a more enabling business environment, the operation has made private investment in Moldova more
attractive. Specifically, by supporting the implementation of OSS and electronic issuance, the operation has
helped reduce the indirect costs of doing business. Indirect costs for businesses (i.e., the management time spent
in obtaining business authorizations and permits was reduced by 42 percent between 2014 and 2020 (equivalent
to annual savings of roughly US$13.5 million).

The Project has also helped mobilize private capital through the MGF, with an estimated US$2.75 million through
the business contribution of beneficiaries of matching grants.20 In addition, the LoC can be expected to have
provided a successful demonstration for PFIs on the potential for long-term lending to export-oriented
enterprises, encouraging PFIs to use their own resources to this type of loans, as illustrated by a 17 percent
increase in their export-oriented portfolios during the operation’s lifetime (KOI 3). Finally, the operation
supported the restructuring of ODIMM’s CGF, which will help further expand lending to MSMEs by banks.

Other Unintended Outcomes and Impacts

This operation provided the basis for collaboration with the International Finance Corporation’s (IFC)’s
Investment Climate Reform Project (ICRP). First, it provided financial support for the feasibility study for the
implementation of an OSS for permits and licenses, which, in turn, provided the baseline for the comprehensive
legal reform supported by the ICRP aimed at eliminating redundant permits, reassessing institutional mandates
and regulatory powers, redefining criteria for issuing permits and licenses, streamlining the issuing procedures,
and establishing a mechanism of control over the “creation” of new permits in the country. Second, it supported
the adoption and implementation of the system of performance indicators for inspection bodies that had been
developed by IFC’s ICRP. The collaboration between the Bank and IFC through these two projects effectively
capitalized on the strengths of the two institutions, generating important synergies for the GoM and providing
an example of good World Bank Group (WBG) practice in the region.


III. KEY FACTORS THAT AFFECTED IMPLEMENTATION AND OUTCOME

A. KEY FACTORS DURING PREPARATION
The operation benefited from a solid design, which reflected a logical results chain, a realistic risk assessment,
and the knowledge acquired in previous Bank engagements. Specific strengths included:

Robust analytical underpinnings – In this operation, the Bank brought to bear its extensive experience in private
sector development issues, both globally and in the ECA region. The operation also built on in-depth Moldova-
specific sector knowledge developed over the preceding decade. The Bank and the GoM had jointly monitored
the business environment for the last fifteen years through the localized Cost of Doing Business Survey (2005-
2021), the key analytical instrument used by the country. The operation also reflected the key recommendations
from other analytical work focusing on Moldova’s competitiveness21 and private sector needs.22

Long-term, multi-pronged Bank engagement - The operation built on the success and knowledge gained during
the implementation of its predecessor—Moldova’s First Competitiveness Enhancement Project (CEP I),23 which
was approved in 2005. The operation also benefited from concurrent Bank support for regulatory reform under
a programmatic series of two Development Policy Operations (DPOs 1 and 2), which helped reinforce the
incentives to align the reform efforts of different government agencies.24 In turn, these two DPOs built on the

B. KEY FACTORS DURING IMPLEMENTATION
Volatile political environment – The political situation in Moldova was unstable during implementation,
particularly during 2015 and 2019. Overall, there were ten different Prime Ministers and nine Ministers of
Economy during the operation’s lifetime. This volatile political environment slowed down implementation, as
reflected in the operation’s Moderately Satisfactory rating during the 2015-2019 period. However, various
factors helped ameliorate the impact at the project level. On the Moldovan side, these factors included: lower
management and technical staff turnaround and strong support at the level of the MoE secretariat responsible
for the project implementation; relative stability within the PIU and the Credit Line Directorate (responsible for
LoC implementation), both of them staffed with highly proactive and technically robust professionals; and strong
consensus on the importance of enhancing the country’s competitiveness across the political spectrum and civil
society resulting, in large part, from strong stakeholder engagement and public consultations. On the Bank side,
these factors included: high visibility of the Bank’s operations focusing on competitiveness; very intense
supervision on the part of the Task Team; and strong and active support by the Country Management Unit (CMU).

Unfavorable external conditions for export growth: Slower import growth in Moldova’s major exports markets
during the operation’s lifetime hindered the expansion of Moldova’s exports. In the case of the EU, which
accounted for 66.7 percent of Moldova’s exports in 2020,28 imports of goods and services increased at an average
annual rate of 3.2 percent between 2015 and 2020 compared to 3. 9 percent between 2010 and 2014. Lower
demand for imports was tied to the EU’s slower GDP growth (i.e., average 1.01 percent per year during the 2015-
2020 period compared to 1.16 during 2010-2014), including the drastic economic contraction resulting from the
COVID-19 pandemic (i.e., almost 6 percent drop in the EU’s GDP in 2020). These unfavorable external conditions,
in turn, slowed down the growth of Moldova’s exports, with the average annual export growth decreasing from

Challenges in the banking sector and the implementation of the LoC – The implementation of the LoC had to
overcome two significant challenges. First, the number of eligible PFIs was significantly reduced because of the
bank fraud scandal that was uncovered in 2014, creating enormous turmoil in the banking sector. The fraud
negatively affected both demand for credit by enterprises and the supply of credit by banks, further shrinking
credit, especially to MSMEs. The three largest banks in the country lost their licenses and were liquidated, and
the next three largest banks were placed under special supervision, making them ineligible for participation as
PFIs in the LoC. The number of PFIs increased gradually during the operation’s lifetime, from two to six between
2014 and 2021. Second, the inflation-based interest rate for sub-loans denominated in local currency under the
LoC was not competitive, 30 as inflation spiked in 2015 as a result of the 2014 banking fraud combined with
spillovers from the Russian economic crisis. This was eventually addressed by adopting a temporary formula for
the interest rate, which remained in place until April 2017.

Other implementation delays – Government restructurings that took place in 2017 and 2020-2021 distracted
attention from Project implementation and slowed down progress. In addition, the level of institutional capacity
and ownership varied across participating government agencies. For example, a more proactive role and strong
ownership of performance indicators would have been needed on the part of the MoE. In this regard, the RBF
provided additional incentives to align the efforts of various government agencies.

COVID-19 pandemic - Moldova was among the countries in Europe most affected by COVID-19, which significantly
impacted households and businesses across the country. The decline in remittances due to the pandemic, which
combined with a severe drought in 2020 resulted in a 7 percent decline in GDP in 2020, underscored the
importance of enhancing the country’s competitiveness to lessen its dependence on remittances. The pandemic
also helped increase the use of the OSS, as reflected in a significant uptake in online applications since the
pandemic started. There were also some negative impacts, including a temporary slowdown in the outreach
activities of ODIMM and the IA as well as OSS implementation and regulatory reforms (given that focus was on
COVID pandemic response), which required a second extension to the operation’s closing date.


IV. BANK PERFORMANCE, COMPLIANCE ISSUES, AND RISK TO DEVELOPMENT OUTCOME

A. QUALITY OF MONITORING AND EVALUATION (M&E)
M&E Design

The operation benefited from a robust M&E framework, which served to gauge progress with respect to outputs,
intermediate results, and impacts (PDOs 1 and 2), all of which were embedded in the Results Chain. The operation’s
impact with respect to PDO 1 was fully captured by KOI 1, while the impact with respect to PDO 2 was captured by

KOIs 2and 4, together with a series of impact evaluations and additional data on export sales and jobs being
systematically collected by the PIU from MGF and LoC beneficiaries. In addition, KOI 3 measured the average
annual percentage increase in overall lending to export-oriented enterprises by PFIs (including but not limited to
the LoC). Although such increase could not be directly attributed to the operation, it was expected to indicate a
possible positive demonstration effect fostering private investments. Finally, four DLIs were included in the M&E
framework to provide additional incentives in support of advancing key regulatory reforms and the institutional
strengthening of the government agencies central to MSME development and exports promotion; thus, increasing
the operation’s focus on results. The PRF was further finetuned under the 2018 restructuring to reflect the
government’s organizational reform and clarify the disbursement triggers under the DLIs.

The Annual Cost of Doing Business surveys done by the GoM under CEP II, which provided inputs for the PRF, were
carried out by external third parties with Project support to complement quantifiable results with the perception
of progress by the business community.

A randomized controlled Impact Evaluation was envisioned at preparation to assess the impact of the MGF on
MSMEs’ outcomes and whether combining matching grants with close monitoring and frequent site visits had a
significant effect.

In retrospect, although the Results Chain had a clear logic, it could have been further improved by having a three-
fold PDO (as opposed to two-fold PDO), connecting each of the three lines of intervention to a separate PDO. This
approach has already been adopted in the design of the follow-on operation, for which the PDO will be “to reduce
the regulatory burden, increase access to finance, and increase the export competitiveness of Moldovan
enterprises.” 31

M&E Implementation

The PIU, which was responsible for the operation’s overall M&E, produced quarterly and annual monitoring and
evaluation reports. These reports were shared with the GoM and the Bank and served as a useful tool for
monitoring Project progress. In addition, it carried out detailed monitoring of project beneficiaries’ performance,
which became a critical complement to the core PRF data.

The MGF Impact Evaluation was conducted as envisioned, with results being reported at three different times
throughout the operation’s lifetime, including: an interim evaluation examining the period between the inception
of the MGF in December 2015 and December 2018; a second interim evaluation covering 2015 to 2019; and the
final impact evaluation covering the entire implementation period (2015 to 2021).32

M&E Utilization

Progress reports tracked progress toward the end-targets and helped in the early identification of deviations and
risks, triggering Action Plans to mitigate them. Annual Cost of Doing Business surveys were used for assessing
progress in the business environment over time as well as the comparison with international benchmarks. Data

B. ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL, AND FIDUCIARY COMPLIANCE
Environmental and Social Safeguards – Project activities were undertaken in a manner consistent with the
operation’s Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), and compliance with environmental and
social safeguards was satisfactory throughout the operation’s lifetime. All grants and subprojects supported under
the operation were subject to environmental and social screening in line with the criteria set out in the operation’s
ESMF. Grants and subprojects that could cause significant impacts (i.e., Category A) were not eligible for Project
financing. Subprojects located in protected areas, critical habitats or culturally or socially sensitive areas, along
with subprojects which might have impacts on international waterways were also excluded from Project financing.
Subprojects were eligible only if they did not require or involve land acquisition.

The social impacts of the project have mostly been positive and the expansion of MSMEs has brought economic
benefits and local employment opportunities. All submitted grants and subprojects were preliminary assessed
from the environmental and social point of view, providing them an environmental category as well as requiring
relevant environmental authorizations, licenses and permits. Also, all approved subprojects had relevant
supporting Environmental Assessment (EA) documentation (e.g., Environmental Screening Checklist and/or
simple Environmental Management Plan, as well as approvals if needed per national legislation). Most grants and
subprojects related to existing facilities and required working capital rather than resources for new activities.
Twenty-one percent of all submitted grants and subprojects fell under Category B, requiring a simple
Environmental Assessment and/or preparation of a simple Environmental Management Plan, while 79 percent
fell under Category C, requiring only environmental due diligence procedure as they were deemed to have an
insignificant environmental impact.

Fiduciary Aspects - Procurement was carried out satisfactorily and in accordance with the applicable Bank
Guidelines. Fiduciary tasks were managed by an experienced PIU. Quality of procurement documents submitted
to the Bank was generally good, with no major issues being identified during ex-post reviews, including at the sub-
grant and sub-loan levels.

Financial Management – The Project’s financial management arrangements were satisfactory throughout the
operation’s lifetime. The internal control system functioned well, and financial reporting was accurate, timely, and
in sufficient details. Walkthrough tests over sample transactions confirmed that they were handled correctly,
accurately documented and properly authorized. Accounting records were kept in automated accounting
software, which allowed full audit trail and proper contract execution monitoring. Audit reports were submitted

including eligible expenditure reports.

C. BANK PERFORMANCE
Quality at Entry

Rating: Satisfactory
The World Bank brought significant value-added to this operation, given the depth and breadth of its expertise
and long-standing engagement with the GoM on private sector development issues. Specifically, preparation
benefited greatly from the Bank’s international experience in advancing private and financial sector development,
particularly in the ECA region. In addition, the Bank was seen as a trusted partner in these reforms both by national
authorities as well as the government agencies tasked with their implementation. Through sustained policy
dialogue, analytical work and a significant portfolio of operations focusing on private sector development and
competitiveness spanning for over two decades, the Bank had acquired in-depth knowledge of the challenges
faced by Moldova as well as the country’s organizational structure, institutional capacities, and legislative and
regulatory frameworks. The operation also built directly on the experience and capacity built during the
implementation of its predecessor, including previous experience in the implementation of regulatory reforms,
ongoing Cost of Doing Business surveys, an already established LoC, as well as a capable PIU with strong
implementation capacity, including environmental safeguards as well as fiduciary and financial management
issues. In addition, the operation benefited from a strong technical design, which included well-defined areas of
intervention under a clear Results Chain, a robust M&E framework and a realistic assessment of Project costs and
implementation risks. The addition of RBF contributed to strengthen the focus on results, creating additional
incentives for the implementation of actions that were central to the success of the reform efforts. While some
adjustments to the PRF were required under the 2019 restructuring (i.e., KOI 4 and IRI 2), these are considered
minor shortcomings.

Quality of Supervision

Rating: Highly Satisfactory
The quality of supervision is deemed highly satisfactory, reflecting the highly satisfactory performance of the Task
Team and the CMU in successfully steering the operation through challenging times during implementation.
Project supervision was in-depth and permanent throughout the operation's lifetime, with regular supervision
missions that included field visits to sub-borrowers to monitor the implementation of the LoC by the PFIs. Findings
were recorded in detailed Aide Memoires and Implementation Status and Results Reports (ISRs). Supervision
efforts intensified significantly at times of high political volatility and when challenges arose in the banking sector
in the post-fraud period, with up to four field supervision missions in a year as well as close implementation
support via videoconferences with the PIU and other Project stakeholders. During those periods, the Task Team
exhibited adequate caution, downgrading the ISR ratings to Moderately Satisfactory to signal the Borrower the
need to address implementation delays. The Mid-Term Review took place in October 2016, a little earlier than
originally anticipated, providing an opportunity to review the PRF, DLIs, and the progress made so far in the
implementation of Project activities. The need to make some minor adjustments to the PRF was identified, which
were subsequently addressed in the 2019 restructuring, together with the need to extend the closing date due to


                                                                                                           Page 24 of 58
   The World Bank
  MD Second Competitiveness Enhancement Project (P144103)



implementation delays. The Task Team monitored the banking sector intensely in order to ensure that only sound
financial institutions joined as PFIs and promptly re-assessed the eligibility of banks to join as PFIs, including one
of the largest banks in Moldova, as soon as it exited the regime of special supervision by the National Bank of
Moldova. Close communication with counterparts, stakeholders, and partners helped identify and address
bottlenecks in a timely manner. The stability and complementary technical skills (i.e., private and financial sector
development) of the two co-Task Team Leaders (TTLs) were important factors contributing to the Project's
successful implementation. The TTLs were also closely involved in the implementation of the MGF Impact
Evaluation, annual Cost of Doing Business surveys, the design, implementation, and dissemination of the 2017
Enterprise Gender Survey, as well as the Bank’s analytical work in Moldova’s financial sector.

The CMU’s performance also contributed to the operation’s implementation success. At the portfolio level, the
CMU was able to effectively articulate different instruments (i.e., analytical work, DPOs and investment projects)
to create a critical mass of incentives to help move forward the reform process. It also maintained a close policy
dialogue with Moldovan authorities, helping ensure continuous support for the strategic priorities of the CPF
despite frequent changes in administration. At the Project level, the CMU also acted proactively, helping overcome
gridlocks and implementation bottlenecks as needed. As an illustration, the GoM addressed the lack of
attractiveness of interest rate under the LoC in the way suggested by the CMU.

Justification of Overall Rating of Bank Performance

Rating: Satisfactory
Overall Bank performance is deemed Satisfactory to reflect its satisfactory performance ensuring quality at entry
and highly satisfactory performance during supervision.

D. RISK TO DEVELOPMENT OUTCOME
Rating: Substantial
The overall risk to the sustainability of the operation’s outcomes is deemed Substantial. Progress made in each of
the three areas of intervention (regulatory reform, access to BDSs, and access to finance) is likely to continue with
sustained political support and continuous Bank support under a US$50 million follow-on operation currently
under preparation (i.e., MSME Competitiveness Project). However, Moldova still presents substantial risks in terms
of political and macroeconomic stability. Favorable and detrimental factors affecting sustainability can be
summarized as follows:

At the country level

Political volatility - Sustained commitment to reform in a country marked by political volatility constitutes a
significant risk for reform momentum. Although in the past frequent political changes did not affect overall
commitment towards foreign investment and export promotion, such changes have the potential to affect policy
priorities. Likewise, future changes in the leadership of key institutions or the instruments selected for policy
implementation might slow down and even reverse the progress made so far.

Macroeconomic risks – Although medium- and long-term macroeconomic risks resulting from the COVID-19
pandemic seem to have been contained, Moldova’s economic outlook is still subject to considerable downside
risks. One of these risks is the unfolding situation in Ukraine. While there is a high level of uncertainty related to

                                                                                                          Page 25 of 58
   The World Bank
  MD Second Competitiveness Enhancement Project (P144103)



the socio-economic ramifications in Moldova, the war in Ukraine is expected to have a substantial negative impact,
including trade, remittances, high energy prices, and heightened financial uncertainties. These risks may negatively
affect demand for BDSs and credit by MSMEs.

At the component level

Regulatory reform – Progress toward regulatory reform has already yielded tangible results, as reflected in the
improvement in the EDB surveys. Regulatory reform has also been supported by legislative actions, which offers a
certain degree of stability. The implementation of policy implementation instruments also exhibits significant
progress. In particular, the OSS system has been integrated into government services, linked to the government’s
e-Payment system and, through MPay, to the private sector. In addition, exceeding what was originally envisioned,
a pilot in the Municipality of Chisinau on improving process and document flows for construction permit processes
on local level was successfully completed, linking all relevant utilities and decision makers in one system. However,
some challenges remain, including ensuring broader adoption of the OSS by issuing authorities, such as the
National Bank of Moldova, which has requested exemption from the OSS system based on its independence.
Likewise, lack of adequate institutional capacity may hinder coordination efforts across multiple institutions for
digitization and other aspects of regulatory reform. Fortunately, these sustainability risks are ameliorated by the
Bank’s continuous support to reform efforts under the follow-on operation, which will provide further support for
digitization of government-to-business services, streamlining of permissive documents, and enhancing the
infrastructure to further reduce regulatory burden.

SME development – Protecting and further expanding the progress made institutional strengthening of ODIMM
and IA will require continuous political and financial support. Lack of institutional memory resulting from frequent
changes in the agencies’ top management and significant turnover of staff, insufficient training, and weak
motivation among staff might also hinder the performance of these agencies, which are central to MSME
development and export promotion.

The follow-on operation will provide support for matching grants, export readiness, supplier linkages, and export
promotion programs, to help ensure the continuous development of MSMEs. Matching grants are now an integral
part of ODIMM’s operations. In addition, a new pilot program to support MSME competitiveness and export
readiness that was developed with support under this operation, has received a US$3 million government
budgetary allocation. In parallel, ODIMM has secured EU funding for the digitization and internationalization of
MSMEs to enhance export readiness.

Access to finance – The LoC was fully disbursed by November 2020, and it is currently managed by a unit within
MoF as a revolving fund, with repayments of principal and interest being collected and reinvested in new similar
private sub-projects, ensuring continuity of the on-lending operations. With Bank support under the follow-on
operation, the GoM will take the next step toward supporting access to finance to MSMEs. Specifically, under the
new operation, the CGF, which was restructured under this operation, will support the provision of portfolio partial
credit guarantees for MSMEs. Given that ODIMM will be responsible for its implementation, ensuring that the
agency has the needed institutional capacity is a necessary condition for its success. The operation will also
enhance ODIMM’s institutional capacity vis-a-vis the CGF.

V. LESSONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
Important lessons can be derived from the implementation of this operation, which can be summarized as
follows:

Robust analytical underpinnings are needed to ensure a relevant and robust Project design. In addition to the
Bank’s extensive experience in private sector development issues, the operation’s design was informed by the in-
depth Moldova-specific sector knowledge acquired over the preceding decade through the regular implementation
of the Cost of Doing Business Survey (2005-2021), as well as the Bank’s analytical work focusing on Moldova’s
competitiveness and private sector needs.

Long-term focused engagement greatly enhances the Bank’s ability to promote development progress. The
operation built on the achievements and knowledge accumulated over more than two decades of the Bank’
engagement with the GoM on competitiveness and private development issues through analytical work and
lending operations. This long-term policy dialogue with the GoM and other stakeholders also helped achieve a
broad consensus on the importance of enhancing the country’s competitiveness as well as the necessary reforms
that had to be undertaken to achieve it.

In times of political turmoil, the Bank can help maintain continuity, both at the policy and operational levels.
Having earned a reputation as a trusted and valuable development partner, the Bank was able to continue the
policy dialogue with Moldovan authorities even during times of rapid turnover at the highest political levels.
Concomitantly, by intensifying supervision and implementation support, the Task Team helped steer the
operation through times of government paralysis during interim governments. In this respect, having a capable
and stable PIU was also critically important in minimizing implementation disruptions.

Multi-pronged Bank engagement greatly enhances the chances of success of individual operations. The
operation benefited from concurrent Bank support for regulatory reform, including a programmatic series of two
DPOs, increasing the incentives to help align the reform efforts of different government agencies. The
implementation was also complemented by TA to financial sector authorities provided by the World Bank and the
WBG’s Financial Sector Advisory Center to strengthen the regulation and supervision framework.

Support for private sector development can have a significant impact in economies that are still transitioning
towards a free-market economy. The significant generation of exports and jobs by the operation’s
beneficiaries illustrates the relevance and effectiveness of providing financial support to enhance enterprises’
access to BDSs and credit, even under unfavorable export conditions.

It is critical to ensure the flexibility in the design of financial intermediation operations. In this regard, it is
important to embed flexibility in the design of LOCs. In the case of this operation, flexible design allowed to reach
a broad set of companies in terms of sectors, geography, and the ability of enterprises to borrow both for
investment and working capital in dollars, Euros or local currency.

Impact evaluations help refine the assessment of the economic impact of a particular intervention. Designed as
a randomized control trial, the impact evaluation of the MGF permitted to isolate the economic impact of the
matching grants, capturing the differences in export sales and new jobs between the treatment and control

                                          ANNEX 4. EFFICIENCY ANALYSIS

Although benefits were not quantified, the economic assessment conducted at Appraisal indicated that the
operation was expected to yield substantial economic benefits by enhancing the business environment and export
competitiveness of Moldovan enterprises, including time savings for the private sector due to reduced regulatory
burden, the generation of new exports, and job creation. First, as noted in the PAD, the regulatory reform
component was expected to significantly enhance the efficiency of government processes and facilitate a business-
enabling environment; thus, reducing the cost of doing business faced by enterprises. It was estimated that, for
the regulatory reform component to have a positive net economic effect, the Net Present Value of the benefit to
firms had to be US$140 per firm due to savings in management time, given that managers spent roughly 28 days
of work per year complying with regulations and all Moldovan enterprises were expected to benefit. Second, the
PAD also anticipated that the MGF was expected to have a significant and positive impact among participating
MSMEs, as a regression analysis of CEP I’s MGF had indicated a positive and statistically significant impact on export
performance two year after the matching grant had been approved. Third, based on the impact assessment of the
LoC supported under the CEP I, it was also anticipated that the access-to-finance component would provide direct
and indirect benefits to Moldovan export-oriented enterprises by providing access to medium- and long-term
financing needed for investments. Specifically, roughly three-fourths of the firms reported a positive effect on their
ability to purchase raw materials in larger quantities and timelier manner and to improve payment terms to
business suppliers. In the case of investment loans, beneficiaries also reported a positive impact on the
modernization of business equipment and/or facilities (e.g., expansion of transportation fleet, installation of new
equipment for sorting and packing seeds, modernization of bottling line, etc.), which, in turn, led to improvements
in the technical efficiency of business operations and expanded production capacity. Finally, LoC loans also had a
positive impact on the composition of the product mix by fostering the development of new products.

The operation’s economic impact is deemed High. In line with what was anticipated at Appraisal, the ex-post
economic analysis that was performed as part of this ICR considering the operation’s actual achievements
confirmed the operation’s significant economic impact. First, by decreasing the time that managers spend dealing
with business permits and other regulations, the operation helped reduce the cost of doing business in Moldova
by approximately US$13.5 million per year, and US$40.4 million over the operation’s lifetime (equivalent to
US$6.95 in savings for each dollar spent under Component 1). Second, by expanding access to BDSs, the operation
has contributed to additional value creation among participating MSMEs, with roughly US$41 million in new export
sales and 1,261 new direct jobs. Of these, approximately US$29.3 million in export sales and 472 new jobs can be
directly attributed to the MGF based on the results of an impact evaluation. Third, by expanding access to finance
among export-oriented enterprises, the operation helped expand exports among borrowing enterprises,
contributing to the generation of US$184 million in cumulative export sales and the creation of 818 new jobs
among LoC final borrowers.

The operation’s implementation efficiency is considered Substantial. The three components were implemented as
originally envisioned in terms of results (i.e., outputs, intermediate outcomes, and impacts) as well as cost
estimates. Although there were implementation delays that required extending the closing date twice for a total
of 23 additional months, these delays were the result of factors outside the Project’s control, such as political
volatility and the COVID-19 pandemic. Despite a very challenging environment, the operation succeeded in
completing all Project activities, disbursing over 95 percent of Project funds, and achieving its PDOs.

Economic Impact

Component 1 – Regulatory Reform: This component supported the GoM’s efforts to improve the business
enabling environment by implementing its regulatory reform strategies. The economic impact of the actions and
activities supported under this component was far reaching, as all Moldovan enterprises benefit from the improved
regulatory processes and reforms. Specifically, it is estimated that the time that managers at Moldovan enterprises
spend dealing with regulatory requirements was reduced from 10.7 percent in 2014 to 6.2 percent in 2021—
equivalent to a 42 percent reduction). Taking into consideration the number of enterprises operating in Moldova
throughout the operation’s lifetime,33 as well as an estimated manager’s salary of US$5,078 per year,34 the time
savings in management time resulting from the operation between 2015 and 202135 would be equivalent to
US$40.4 million (see Table 4.1). Given that the actual cost of Component 1 was US$5.81 million, the resulting
Benefit-Cost ratio equals to 6.95, which indicates the high economic efficiency of investments aimed at promoting
and implementing regulatory reforms. Moreover, it is important to underscore that this measure of economic
efficiency is extremely conservative, as the economic benefits resulting from the time savings will continue in the
future, equivalent to roughly US$13.5 million per year assuming these reductions are maintained constant. In
addition, management could reorient time savings toward strategic business activities that could yield additional
benefits in the form of higher revenue, lower costs, and higher overall productivity.

This component also provided support for the institutional strengthening of ODIMM and IA, which are

Component 3 - Access to Finance: The LoC was conceived to provide funding to intermediary local financial
institutions for on-lending to export-oriented private enterprises to enhance their access to financing for working
capital and investment. The LoC was administered by the Credit Line Directorate under the Ministry of Finance,
an autonomous entity entrusted with the management of various external credit lines for development.
Launched in 2014 with a total funding of US$29.4 million, the LoC was fully disbursed by November 2020. It
continues to be managed by the Credit Line Directorate as a revolving fund, with repayments of principal and
interest are collected and reinvested in new similar private sub-projects, ensuring continuity of the on-lending
operations.

Investment loans were capped at US$800,000, while working capital loans cannot exceed US$500,000. Maximum
maturities are eight years for investment loans and four years for working capital operations. Loans were
denominated in MDL or in foreign currency, Euros or US dollars. On-lending LoC funds were channeled through
six eligible domestic PFIs. As of end 2020, a total of 157 loans had been disbursed to 131 enterprises, with an
average of about US$225,000 per enterprise. Most loans fall in the US$50,000 - 500,000 range, while a handful
number of loans exceeded US$500,000 and a similar number was below US$50,000.

More than half of LoC beneficiaries were active in agro-business, with 61 percent of sub-loans being provided to
producers (e.g., tractors, harvesters, sorting equipment, seeds, fertilizers, etc.), with a significant proportion of
beneficiaries (23 percent of the total loans amount approved) investing in cold storage facilities. Based on the
average cost and storage capacity of a cooling facility, it is estimated that the LoC provided financing for the
construction of 47,000 tons of storage capacity throughout the country.41 In addition, 36 percent of sub-loans

Implementation Efficiency

The operation’s implementation efficiency is deemed Substantial. The three components were implemented as
originally envisioned in terms of results (i.e., outputs, intermediate outcomes, and impacts) as well as the costs,
which showed almost no deviation from the original estimates, both in amount as well as disbursement
categories. Although there were implementation delays that required extending the closing date twice for a total
of 23 additional months, these delays were due to factors beyond the control of the operation, including a volatile
political environment and the COVID-19 pandemic. Despite these delays, the operation succeeded in the full
completion of Project activities, 95 percent disbursements and remarkable PDO achievement amidst a very
challenging implementation environment.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY43

The Government of the Republic of Moldova (GoM) is pursuing a policy agenda to support export-led economic
growth. To achieve this goal, the Moldova 2020 national development strategy, approved in 2012, focused on
improving the business enabling environment and promoting better access to finance for enterprises, as two of
its top priorities.

The Second Competitiveness Enhancement Project (CEP II or “the Project”) was closely aligned with GoM’s policy
priorities as identified in the Moldova 2020 strategy, the Roadmap for Increasing Competitiveness, the Business
Roadmap, the Regulatory Reform Strategy 2013-2020, and SME Development Strategy 2012-2015. These
strategies highlighted the priorities of improving the business enabling environment, enterprise
competitiveness, and access to finance.

Objectives. The CEP II supported the Government of Moldova’s efforts to promote economic growth and job
creation. In a context of low investment levels, limited productivity and poor export competitiveness, the Project
aimed at enhancing Moldova’s private sector activity and investment. The Project development objective was
to “increase the export competitiveness of Moldovan enterprises and decrease the regulatory burden they
face.”44 The PDO was achieved through a set of measures that aim to: (i) improve the business environment
through regulatory reforms that reduce the cost of doing business; (ii) help SMEs and exporters to get access to
business development services; and (iii) improve access to medium- and long-term finance for export-oriented
enterprises.

All planned Project Development Objectives have been met or exceeded. In addition, Government reforms and
Project-supported activities generated 2,079 jobs, more than US$225 million in new exports, an estimated
US$15 million in annual savings for the private sector.

Timeline. The Project was approved by the World Bank Board of Directors in May 2014 and ratified by the
Parliament in July 2014.45 The Project became effective on October 2, 2014, with completion date set initially
for January 30, 2020, with following two extensions,46 the first one until July 30, 2021, and the second one until
December 31, 2021. The final closing date of the Project was December 31, 2021.

Components. The Project included four components, focusing on (i) support to implementation of the regulatory
reform to ensure a transparent, predictable, and low-cost business enabling environment for private sector
operations; (ii) the development of Small and Medium Enterprise sector through strengthening the institutional

A short description and results of each component is summarized below:

       (i)   Regulatory Reform. The component was designed to bring about significant reform in efficiency of
             government processes and facilitate a business-enabling environment. The activities undertaken under
             this component aimed to reduce the cost of doing business faced by SMEs, by improving the efficiency
             of government processes used to regulate of private sector activity, and thereby reducing the cost to
             businesses of interactions with public agencies. As key activities under this component were (i) building
             institutional capacity for regulation, and implementing Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA) in the
             legislative process of Moldova; (ii) establishing and implementing a system that strengthens
             accountability and incentives for public authorities that regulate business activities; (iii) streamlining
             the processes for obtaining permissive documents for Moldovan exporters through implementation of
             One-Stop Shop electronic platform, as well as legal reforms to reduce overlaps and duplicates; (iv)
             support provided to Competition Council in tackling anti-competitive market conduct and proactively
             advocating for fair competition in the economy through reviewing the laws and regulations to exclude
             barriers to competition and in implementing its competition policy through better tools and processes;
             and (vii) support to State Chancellery in upgrading the functionalities of the State Inspection e-Registry
             and making its use mandatory, as the only way of performing business inspections. All activities under
             this component were focused on the implementation of the Regulatory Reform Strategy and its Action
             Plan 2013-2020, approved by the GoM in 2013; and the Roadmap for Increasing Competitiveness,
             approved by the government in 2014 and ensure sustainability of their outcomes. Main responsible
             agency for this component was the Ministry of Economy in collaboration with State Chancellery, Public
             Service Agency, E-Government Agency, and Competition Council.

       (ii) Small and Medium Enterprise Development. The objective of the SME Development component was
            to strengthen Moldovan SMEs’ linkages to markets and ability to compete in those markets. The
            objective was achieved through two closely related aspects: (a) enhancing the institutional capacity of
            ODIMM and MIEPO48, so that they can play a more effective role in facilitating SMEs’ export growth and
            (b) providing matching grants to SMEs to implement business improvement projects focused on export
            competitiveness. As for ODIMM, the goal was to transform the organization from a start-up-oriented
            agency, into SME support agency, including export readiness. In 2015, ODIMM was an agency focusing
            primarily on start-ups and micro entrepreneurs and has steadily changed the loop, focusing on SMEs,
            exports, and value chains, focusing on green economy, and to smaller extent on startups. New programs
            on SME competitiveness and export readiness, internationalization, green transition, as well as the
            Credit Guarantee Fund, are some of the initiatives and programs developed during last years and
            supported by the Project along with establishing the dedicated internationalization department at

          Matching Grants Facility (MGF). Through the MGF, the project helped 272 Moldovan SMEs to get access
          to business development services (BDS) and equipment. Service providers supported SMEs to, inter
          alia: (i) improve existing products and services; (ii) create new products and services; (iii) improve
          production processes; (iv) improve business management; (v) improve business image; (vi) find new
          customers and markets; and (vii) create and strengthen partnerships within the value chain. MGF with
          a budget of US$ 2.7 million was fully committed, while by Project completion date a rate of 91% of
          proceeds or USD 2.5 million have been offered to recipients. A total of US$41 million in new export
          sales have been realized by MGF recipients so far. This is a significant result of the program.

      (iii) Access to Finance. This component contributed to improving access to medium and long-term finance
            for export-oriented enterprises, reducing barriers to finance due to perceived high credit risk in SME
            finance and high collateral requirements, and promoting suitable models for value chain financing,
            particularly in the agriculture sector. To achieve the above objective, a Line of credit has been
            implemented to provide medium to long term financing for working capital and investment purposes,
            to export-oriented enterprises and technical assistance to revamp the existing credit guarantee scheme.
            The resources of the CEP II Credit Line in the amount of US $29.4 million have been fully assimilated by
            the end of November 2020 being approved and financed 157 projects disbursed to 131 private export-
            oriented companies. About 68% of the total amount was allocated for investment projects cumulated
            with related working capital and 71.2% of the approved loans were provided to direct exporters and
            around 75 percent are medium and long-term loans (more than 36 months). According to PFI reports,
            the bulk of the sub-loans (more than 95%) were provided to agricultural and agro-processing
            enterprises. In six years of Credit Line implementation period (2015-2020), final borrowers have
            reported cumulative export revenues totaling US$184 million and 818 new jobs created. The Office for
            Managing External Assistance Programs (former Credit Line Directorate) is managing the reflow fund
            and applies the same eligibility criteria for new applications from PFIs.

      (iv) Project Management. The project was implemented by the PIU under the MoE with the support of the
           Credit Line Directorate49 under the MoF. The Project Management component covered the PIU’s cost
           of managing Component 1 and Component 2 of the project, as well as activities that overlap with
           Component 3 (e.g., accountant, procurement specialist, environmental specialist, etc.) The key activities
           under the project management component were related to procurement, financial management,
           audits, and other activities related to overall project management, monitoring and coordination.

Results-Based Financing (RBF): The project benefited from an innovative RBF intervention (direct budgetary
support) in the amount of US$2.7 million to set and publicly report results, encourage accountability, and
improve performance under the Regulatory reform and SME development components. The disbursement rate
of RBF at the project completion date is 96%. RBF has been set aside for compliance with 4 Disbursement Linked
Indicators (DLI) that reflect the government’s own objectives and are deemed highly relevant to the success of
regulatory reform. Only one sub-indicator related to amendments to the competition law was not met under
CEP II. Due to the prolonged process of consultations and Government changes, the amendments have not been
adopted in due time before Project completion. However, the Government is committed to this reform, and
amendments to the Competition Law are considered as a prior action for the new Development Policy Operation
currently prepared by the World Bank, thus showing renewed interest and commitment for the reform.

abstract: Ratings for the Second Competitiveness Enhancement Project for Moldova were as follows: outcome was highly satisfactory, Bank performance was satisfactory, and monitoring and evaluation (M and E) quality was high. Some lessons learned included: robust analytical underpinnings are needed to ensure a relevant and robust project design. Long-term focused engagement greatly enhances the Bank’s ability to promote development progress. In times of political turmoil, the Bank can help maintain continuity, both at the policy and operational levels. Multi-pronged Bank engagement greatly enhances the chances of success of individual operations. Support for private sector development can have a significant impact in economies that are still transitioning towards a free-market economy. It is critical to ensure the flexibility in the design of financial intermediation operations. Impact evaluations help refine the assessment of the economic impact of a particular intervention.
